one_line_takeaway: dgMARK watermarks discrete diffusion language models by steering which token position is unmasked next (rather than biasing token probabilities), achieving detectability competitive with or exceeding KGW and PATTERN-MARK baselines at lower perplexity cost, with a beam-search lookahead variant needed to reach the strongest detection rates.
key_finding: Table 1 (standard dgMARK, z=4.0 threshold): on C4, FPR=0.000/TPR=0.957 (LLaDA, PPL 4.90), FPR=0.000/TPR=0.929 (LLaDA 1.5, PPL 5.27), FPR=0.000/TPR=0.958 (Dream, PPL 5.75); on Writing Prompts, FPR=0.000/TPR=0.983 (LLaDA, PPL 6.00), FPR=0.004/TPR=0.733 (LLaDA 1.5, PPL 6.34), FPR=0.007/TPR=0.682 (Dream, PPL 6.87) -- showing detectability is markedly weaker on Writing Prompts for LLaDA 1.5 and Dream. Table 2 (LLaDA 1.5 on C4) shows dgMARK+3-beam reaches TPR=0.963 (greedy) / 1.000 (multinomial) with PPL 4.75/5.40, versus non-watermarked baseline PPL 4.03/4.21 -- a smaller perplexity increase than KGW(delta=3) (PPL 5.83/7.87, TPR 0.970/0.984) or PATTERN-MARK(delta=3) (PPL 5.86/7.69, TPR 0.584/0.586) at comparable or lower detectability cost; increasing beam width from 1 to 8 monotonically raises TPR and TPR@FPR while only modestly raising PPL. Table 3 benchmark results (matched-detectability delta=3 for baselines) show dgMARK causes the smallest downstream-accuracy degradation: e.g., on LLaDA greedy sampling, non-watermarked MMLU/GSM8K/HumanEval = 0.648/0.797/0.427 versus dgMARK 0.647/0.787/0.280 versus KGW 0.558/0.662/0.092 and PATTERN-MARK 0.570/0.635/0.134; HumanEval (low-entropy code generation) shows the largest degradation across all methods including dgMARK. Robustness figures report AUC dropping from 0.999 (epsilon=0) to 0.807 (epsilon=0.4 random deletion) for dgMARK, and detectability persists under DIPPER and LLaMA-3-8B paraphrasing, though weakening as paraphrase intensity increases (exact AUC/TPR numbers under paraphrasing are given only in figures, not reproduced as a table in the main text excerpt reviewed).
core_contribution: The paper introduces dgMARK, a watermarking method for discrete diffusion language models (dLLMs, e.g., LLaDA, Dream) that embeds a detectable signal purely by steering the decoding (unmasking) order, rather than by reweighting/biasing the model's token probabilities as prior LLM watermarks (e.g., KGW's green/red list biasing) do. At each decoding step, a deterministic binary hash keyed on a secret watermark key ξ partitions the vocabulary at each position i into a 'parity-matching' set G_i and residual R_i; dgMARK prioritizes unmasking positions whose currently predicted candidate token already falls in G_i (falling back to all remaining positions if none qualify), then picks the highest-reward such position under the underlying decoding heuristic (confidence, entropy, or margin). This is wrapped around standard dLLM decoding (Algorithm 2, contrasted with generic decoding in Algorithm 1) and requires no logit modification. The authors also propose a one-step lookahead / top-k beam-search variant (k in {1,3,5,8}) that greedily reorders among parity-matching candidates by estimating, for each candidate, how many further positions would remain parity-matching after committing to it (score g(j)), trading extra computation for stronger embedding. Detection uses a global z-test on the fraction of tokens whose value lies in the position-indexed parity set (baseline rate 1/2 under H0, z = (G - n/2)/sqrt(n/4)), plus a sliding-window two-sided aggregated statistic (z_win, averaging squared per-window z-scores) designed to remain sensitive to local parity deviations even when insertions/deletions shift token indices.
threat_model: The setting is standard LLM-watermarking provenance: a model owner wants to later verify, using a secret key, whether a given text was generated by their dLLM, without needing model access or the original prompt at detection time, and while avoiding the detectability-quality trade-off that plagues probability-biasing watermarks. The paper explicitly treats an adversary who tries to strip or evade the watermark via post-editing: random token-level insertion, deletion, and substitution at perturbation budgets epsilon in {0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4}, and paraphrasing attacks using DIPPER-11B (two configurations, DIPPER-1 and DIPPER-2 with added 10% order diversity) and LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct prompted to paraphrase. The paper does not model an adversary who knows the hash function/key or who has white-box access to steer around the watermark (i.e., no adaptive/key-aware attacker is considered), nor does it address multi-key collusion or forgery/spoofing of the watermark.
limitations: The authors explicitly state their claim is limited: dgMARK does not preserve the model's exact output distribution (it only avoids *directly* modifying learned conditional probabilities via logit biasing/renormalization/green-red boosting) -- it can and does implicitly shift the output distribution through proposal selection and decoding-order preference, and the paper is careful to distinguish this from true distortion-freeness. Detectability is materially weaker on Writing Prompts than C4 for two of three models in Table 1 (TPR drops to 0.733 and 0.682 for LLaDA 1.5 and Dream respectively), and greedy sampling without beam search shows meaningfully lower TPR (0.540, 60.96% TPR@0.01%FPR) than with beam search, meaning the strong headline numbers depend on the more expensive lookahead variant. HumanEval/code-generation quality degrades more than MMLU/GSM8K for all watermarking methods including dgMARK, attributed to low output entropy limiting available parity-matching alternatives. Beam search variants trade increased computation for stronger embedding, and the paper does not report wall-clock/compute overhead numbers in the sections reviewed. The threat model does not consider an adversary with knowledge of the hash function or key, nor spoofing/forgery attacks, nor collusion across multiple watermarked outputs. Method requires access to model's per-position reward/decoding-strategy outputs at generation time (i.e., it is a generation-side technique for the model owner, not an externally-applicable retrofit).

quantitative_results: Table 1, C4/LLaDA: FPR=0.000, TNR=1.000, TPR=0.957, FNR=0.043, PPL=4.90 (z=4.0 threshold)
Table 1, C4/LLaDA 1.5: FPR=0.000, TPR=0.929, FNR=0.071, PPL=5.27
Table 1, C4/Dream: FPR=0.000, TPR=0.958, FNR=0.042, PPL=5.75
Table 1, WritingPrompts/LLaDA: FPR=0.000, TPR=0.983, FNR=0.017, PPL=6.00
Table 1, WritingPrompts/LLaDA 1.5: FPR=0.004, TPR=0.733, FNR=0.267, PPL=6.34
Table 1, WritingPrompts/Dream: FPR=0.007, TPR=0.682, FNR=0.318, PPL=6.87
Table 2 (LLaDA 1.5, C4, greedy sampling): dgMARK PPL=4.44, TPR=0.540; +3-beam PPL=4.75, TPR=0.963; +5-beam PPL=5.01, TPR=0.987; +8-beam PPL=5.16, TPR=0.991
Table 2 (LLaDA 1.5, C4, multinomial sampling): dgMARK PPL=5.27, TPR=0.929; +3-beam PPL=5.40, TPR=1.000; +5-beam PPL=5.76, TPR=1.000; +8-beam PPL=6.00, TPR=1.000
Table 3 (LLaDA, greedy): non-watermarked MMLU/GSM8K/HumanEval=0.648/0.797/0.427; dgMARK=0.647/0.787/0.280; dgMARK+3-beam=0.647/0.771/0.268
Robustness (random deletion, dgMARK): AUC=0.999 at epsilon=0.0, 0.988 at epsilon=0.1, 0.928 at epsilon=0.2, 0.857 at epsilon=0.3, 0.807 at epsilon=0.4
baselines_compared: KGW (Kirchenbauer et al., 2023) at delta in {1,2,3}
PATTERN-MARK (Chen et al., 2025) at delta in {1,2,3}
